source_item.title: Government may roll out flexible pension scheme for all workers under EPFO 3.0 reforms: Report

hook.label: India's **Biggest** Pension Shake-Up Is Here
hook.subhead: Gig workers may finally get a retirement plan.
hook.image_scene: A Swiggy delivery rider on a motorbike at a busy city intersection, helmet on, phone mount visible on the handlebar, shot from street level at dusk.

beats.label: THE PROBLEM
beats.headline: **76%** of India's Workers Have No Pension
beats.body: India has 55 crore workers. Nearly 42 crore of them work in the unorganised sector and get nothing when they retire.
beats.image_scene: An elderly construction labourer sitting on a half-built wall eating lunch from a tiffin box, safety helmet resting beside him, shot in the afternoon glare.

stat.caption: workers with little or no pension cover

beats.label: THE OLD RULE
beats.headline: Old System Only Covered the **Formal Few**
beats.body: EPFO's pension scheme, running since 1995, covers only workers at companies with 20-plus employees earning below Rs 15,000 a month. Everyone else is out.
beats.image_scene: A corporate office lobby with a security badge scanner at the entrance, suited employees swiping in, shot from outside the glass doors.

beats.label: WHAT'S CHANGING
beats.headline: EPFO 3.0 Wants to Cover **Everyone**
beats.body: The government is designing a new contributory pension scheme for gig workers, unorganised workers, construction workers, and even higher-wage employees currently left out.
beats.image_scene: A wide shot of a busy vegetable market with vendors, loaders, and a food delivery bike all in the same frame, capturing the variety of informal work.

beats.label: HOW IT WORKS
beats.headline: Save Now, Pick Your Pension **at 55**
beats.body: You save into your own digital account until age 55. At that point you choose how to draw it down. At 60, the "Target Retirement Sum" converts into a monthly pension based on actual returns.
beats.image_scene: A person's hands holding a smartphone showing a savings progress bar or retirement corpus tracker app, blurred background of a kitchen table.

beats.label: WHO PUTS IN THE MONEY
beats.headline: Swiggy and Uber Could **Chip In** Too
beats.body: The fund would draw from workers, employers, government top-ups for low earners, and platform companies contributing 1-2% of their annual turnover.
beats.image_scene: The Zomato or Swiggy logo on a food delivery bag hanging off a parked motorcycle outside a restaurant, shot tight and close-up.

beats.label: NOT DONE YET
beats.headline: Still a Proposal. **No Sign-Off** Yet.
beats.body: The scheme is at an early stage with no formal government approval. The Ministry has not even decided which agency will run it.
beats.image_scene: An empty government meeting room with files stacked on a table and a whiteboard with diagrams, shot from the doorway.

engage.label: YOUR TAKE
engage.headline: Should gig platforms like Swiggy and Zomato be **forced to pay** into your pension?
engage.cta: Drop your take below. Follow for more policy stories that affect your money.
engage.image_scene: A delivery rider checking his phone while leaning against his bike outside an apartment building at night, the building's lit lobby visible behind him.
